title: Introduction to SQL

theory.introduction: SQL (Structured Query Language) is a standard programming language for managing and manipulating relational databases. It allows you to store, retrieve, update, and delete data in a database.

sections.title: What is a Database?
sections.content:
A database is an organized collection of structured information, or data, typically stored electronically in a computer system. A relational database stores data in tables with rows and columns.

Key Concepts:

  • Table: A collection of related data organized in rows and columns
  • Row (Record): A single entry in a table
  • Column (Field): A specific attribute or characteristic of the data
  • Primary Key: A unique identifier for each row
  • Foreign Key: A field that links to a primary key in another table

sections.title: SQL Language Categories
sections.content:
SQL commands are grouped into four major categories:

  • DQL (Data Query Language): SELECT - retrieving data from databases
  • DML (Data Manipulation Language): INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE - modifying data
  • DDL (Data Definition Language): CREATE, ALTER, DROP - defining database structure
  • DCL (Data Control Language): GRANT, REVOKE - controlling access to data

sections.title: Basic SELECT Syntax
sections.content:
The SELECT statement is used to query data from a database table. Here's the basic syntax:

SELECT column1, column2, ...
FROM table_name;

You can also select all columns using the asterisk (*) wildcard:

SELECT * FROM table_name;

examples.title: Select all columns
examples.description: Retrieve all data from the countries table

examples.title: Select specific columns
examples.description: Retrieve only the name and population columns

exercise.instruction: Write a query to select all columns from the cities table to see all the cities in our database.
exercise.hint: Use SELECT * to get all columns, and specify the table name after FROM.

learningObjectives: Understand what SQL is and its purpose
Learn basic database terminology (table, row, column)
